title: Reveal Lab Incident Response Scenario
markdown:
Incident Response Scenario:

You're a digital forensic analyst working for a top-tier financial institution. Your organization's SIEM system has alerted you to suspicious behavior originating from an internal workstation that has privileged access to confidential financial information. This activity could signal a potential cyber intrusion.

A **memory dump** from the affected machine has been secured for analysis. Your mission is to investigate this dump for **indicators of compromise (IoCs)**, determine how the anomaly started, contain the threat, and evaluate the extent of the incident.

> 💡 **Tip:** Don’t forget to use hints when stuck—they can guide your investigation!

* * *

#### 🛠 Tools Required:

- **Volatility3**

**📥 Installing Volatility3:**

Copy

```
git clone https://github.com/volatilityfoundation/volatility3.git
cd volatility3
pip3 install -r requirements.txt
```

* * *

### 🔍 Forensic Analysis Tasks

* * *

#### **Q1) Identify the malicious process**

**Question:** Spotting the rogue process is the first step in understanding the attack. What is the name of the suspicious process?

**Solution:**
Use the `windows.pslist` plugin to get an overview of running processes:

Copy

```
python3 vol.py -f /home/kali/Downloads/192-Reveal.dmp windows.pslist
```

![](https://omar-4.gitbook.io/omar-khalid/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F1803975356-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FoCigElGty18x5rSkroXb%252Fuploads%252FB20e13GQ7yciztJC8GQP%252FScreenshot%25202025-07-04%2520131300.png%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3Dd99e72fe-f6ca-4178-9c09-af9a23ea977f&width=768&dpr=3&quality=100&sign=67652bc0&sv=2)

Among the entries, you might notice an unusual instance of `powershell.exe`, which is out of place in this context. That’s your malicious process.

**Answer:**`powershell.exe`

* * *

#### **Q2) Find the parent PID**

**Question:** Discovering the PPID (Parent Process ID) helps map out the process hierarchy and understand how the threat initiated.

![](https://omar-4.gitbook.io/omar-khalid/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F1803975356-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FoCigElGty18x5rSkroXb%252Fuploads%252FvnK9ni2lnKRn8qW8MBzH%252FScreenshot%25202025-07-04%2520130120.png%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3Dd2169187-764a-4a99-8d2c-5fccb26c8328&width=768&dpr=3&quality=100&sign=86669d0c&sv=2)

**Answer:**`4120`

* * *

#### **Q3) Identify the second-stage payload filename**

**Question:** Knowing what executable was used to deploy the second payload helps pinpoint the next phase of the attack.

> 🔎 Hint: Try using the `windows.cmdline` plugin.

Copy

```
python3 vol.py -f /home/kali/Downloads/192-Reveal.dmp windows.cmdline
```

Look for the command line arguments tied to `powershell.exe`. You’ll likely spot a DLL being loaded:

![](https://omar-4.gitbook.io/omar-khalid/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F1803975356-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FoCigElGty18x5rSkroXb%252Fuploads%252Fef5wH9OO0jwfBSeIFsAj%252FScreenshot%25202025-07-04%2520130205.png%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3D2351d76c-3dce-4405-abd3-6c00c00d9bd2&width=768&dpr=3&quality=100&sign=c8fbf066&sv=2)

**Answer:**`3435.dll`

* * *

#### **Q4) What is the remote shared directory accessed?**

**Question:** Tracing which shared resource the attacker accessed remotely can reveal exfiltration points or staging areas.

![](https://omar-4.gitbook.io/omar-khalid/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F1803975356-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FoCigElGty18x5rSkroXb%252Fuploads%252Fxj5ClNqCmpl30GiazDf1%252FScreenshot%25202025-07-04%2520130235.png%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3D1e9a2240-1898-4d1b-bba8-7692717bf4f0&width=768&dpr=3&quality=100&sign=eee3d8b2&sv=2)

**Answer:**`davwwwroot`

* * *

#### **Q5) What MITRE technique was used?**

**Question:** Based on the process command line (hint: includes `rundll32`), what technique ID does this correspond to in the MITRE ATT&CK framework?

> `rundll32` is commonly abused to bypass security mechanisms.

![](https://omar-4.gitbook.io/omar-khalid/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F1803975356-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FoCigElGty18x5rSkroXb%252Fuploads%252FK0xmGkKCVt6b5HuJzf21%252FScreenshot%25202025-07-04%2520130434.png%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3D6a2c212f-0735-484f-874d-41720efa5e91&width=768&dpr=3&quality=100&sign=596ab62&sv=2)

**Answer:**`T1218.011`

* * *

#### **Q6) What is the compromised user account?**

**Question:** Determining which user account executed the malicious process helps assess which credentials and permissions were abused.

> Use: `windows.session` plugin

Copy

```
python3 vol.py -f /home/kali/Downloads/192-Reveal.dmp windows.session
```

![](https://omar-4.gitbook.io/omar-khalid/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F1803975356-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FoCigElGty18x5rSkroXb%252Fuploads%252FxRuYA4QMU6DfbVey4C7j%252FScreenshot%25202025-07-04%2520130325.png%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3D2c5c3bc6-7329-4980-9c07-2d2b6d950be5&width=768&dpr=3&quality=100&sign=8729bcc9&sv=2)

**Answer:**`Elon`

* * *

#### **Q7) Identify the malware family**

**Question:** Based on the suspicious IP address or artifacts found, you investigate on VirusTotal. What is the known malware family name associated?

Head to the **Graph Summary** in VirusTotal and expand related nodes.

![](https://omar-4.gitbook.io/omar-khalid/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F1803975356-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FoCigElGty18x5rSkroXb%252Fuploads%252FgZy5lmZlfXxiSbxLOCiX%252FScreenshot%25202025-07-04%2520130404.png%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3Dddc074c8-02ad-4d2f-9795-2c0fa4892c3d&width=768&dpr=3&quality=100&sign=517a075b&sv=2)

**Answer:**`StrelaStealer`

* * *

#### 🔗 Lab Source:

[**CyberDefenders – Reveal**](https://cyberdefenders.org/blueteam-ctf-challenges/reveal/)
